w:r.w:t: HOUSE BRAIN
Fabricator Robot Blueprint
Refined from C2A Transmutation Chain | v1
Core Doctrine:
Borrow the Earth's mass for compression force. Let Verification do the quality thinking. Fix the geometry, tune the fill — never the other way around.
One-Sentence Summary:

The Fabricator Robot is a ground-anchored, earth-pressing machine that ingests the Excavator's coarse-qualified soil feed, conditions it minimally, compresses it to a fixed geometry using the Earth itself as the compression anvil, and tunes fill volume dynamically via Verification feedback until the calibration loop converges on a stable, spec-conforming block.
1. Purpose
The Fabricator Robot is the machine that turns raw earth feed into usable structural blocks.
It sits between the Excavator (which liberates and coarse-qualifies soil) and Verification (which certifies the finished block). Its job is purely mechanical and process-oriented.
It is not trying to be:
A soil scientist
A quality inspector
A structural engineer
Ingest coarse earth feed. Condition minimally. Compress to correct geometry. Hand off to Verification.
2. Where the Fabricator Starts
Unlike other robots in the system, the Fabricator's constraint space was already 70% resolved by the time it was defined — because every other robot's boundary had already been drawn.
The known constraints entering design:
Input is fixed: Excavator hands it a coarse-qualified soil feed via surface sorting container. Fabricator does not dig.
Output is fixed: Verification is waiting to certify the finished block against the House Brain's recipe. Fabricator does not self-diagnose quality.
Material is fixed: In-situ resource utilization. The ground the house sits on is the primary structural material. Zero supply chain.
The only open problem was the mechanics of turning that earth into a spec-conforming block — cheaply, reliably, at field scale.
3. End-to-End Simple Analogy
Think of baking cookies with a cookie-cutter press.
The House Brain is the recipe: this shape, this thickness, this density.
The Excavator is the flour mill: it delivers coarse-qualified raw material.
The Fabricator is the person pressing each cookie to the exact same size and handing it to the oven.
Verification is the oven and the quality checker: it confirms the cookie came out right.

The hard part is not the pressing itself. The hard part is that the flour changes every batch — sometimes dry and powdery, sometimes clumpy and humid. If you press to a fixed pressure, the cookies come out different sizes. If you press to a fixed size, the thin batches come out crumbly.
The transmutation:
Always press to the fixed size. Then adjust how much flour goes into the mold based on what Verification says about the last cookie. The size never changes. The fill volume does.
4. Final C2A Logic
The final doctrine of Fabricator:
Do not try to generate compression force from robot mass alone
Anchor into the Earth and borrow its mass as the anvil
Always press to a fixed geometry — Assembly depends on dimensional consistency
Adjust fill volume, not target size, to compensate for variable soil density
Confirm anchor seating before every compression cycle — not assumed
Detect incoming moisture before pressing — apply minimum conditioning only
Let Verification close the quality loop — Fabricator learns from feedback, not from onboard sensors
Convergence limit protects against bad soil patches that can never calibrate
First 3–5 blocks of any new soil patch are calibration blocks — expected and planned for
Feed quality data back to Excavator so its gate improves over time
5. Constraints and Your Transmutations
Constraint 1 — The Hydraulic Leviathan Problem
Problem:

To make a Compressed Earth Block that won't dissolve in the rain, you must squeeze dirt with 1,000–3,000 PSI. Traditional CEB machines solve this by being massive, heavy, diesel-powered hydraulic presses that weigh several tons. A lightweight cheap robot that tries to push down on a mold with 3,000 PSI of force will just lift itself off the ground or tear its own chassis apart.
Simple analogy:

Trying to crush a soda can with your hands while it floats in the air — you won't crush it, you'll just push yourself upward. But if the can sits on a solid table, you can press down and crush it. The table resists for you.
Transmutation: The Earth Anvil — Borrow the Ground's Mass

Why not use the ground itself? Instead of robot weight resisting the press force, the ground resists the press force. Drive ground anchors in, lock the robot to the Earth, then compress between the anchor and the press plate. The Earth is already infinitely heavy.
Result:
Robot drives ground screws or expanding friction anchors before any compression cycle
Compression force path: anchor → Earth → press plate — not through the robot chassis
Robot mass no longer limits maximum achievable press force
Cheap, high-torque ball screw or toggle joint replaces multi-ton hydraulic press frame
Same principle the Survey Robot uses: make the contact zone do the work, not the whole machine
Constraint 2 — The Wet Flour Problem
Problem:

The Assembly Robot needs every block geometrically perfect to place it blindly using motor impedance. Verification needs every block at the correct density. Pressing to a fixed pressure produces consistent density but inconsistent size. Pressing to a fixed size produces consistent geometry but inconsistent density. The Fabricator is a cheap robot with no onboard soil lab.
Simple analogy:
If the flour is dry and powdery one day, and clumpy and humid the next, following the exact same recipe produces cookies of totally different sizes and textures.
Transmutation: Dynamic Fill Volume — Fix the Size, Tune the Fill

If we can't change the characteristics of the flour we can manipulate its shape. We will use a reference point — the first block is what is used. Every block made after must be within a threshold. The fill volume adjusts, not the target size. Verification communicates this back to Fabricator and by then Verification has enough data to guide it reliably.
Result:
Target geometry is always fixed — Assembly's dimensional requirement is the hard constraint
Fill volume is the variable — more soil crammed into the same mold space = higher density
If Verification reports block too light: increase fill volume for next cycle
If Verification reports block too dense or overloaded: reduce fill volume
First 3–5 blocks of each new soil patch are calibration blocks — expected, recycled, not failures
System accepts calibration waste as the cost of soil variability — explicitly planned, not improvised
6. Refinements
Five gaps identified after blueprint review. Each adds a safety boundary, a sensing layer, a handling protocol, or a cross-system data path the original two-constraint design left open.
Refinement 1 — The Calibration Spiral Problem
Gap identified:

The blueprint establishes that the first 3–5 blocks of any new soil patch are calibration blocks — they get rejected and recycled. But there is no convergence limit. A soil patch that keeps producing out-of-spec blocks after 10 iterations is not a calibration problem — it is a soil problem. Without a limit, a bad soil batch can consume the entire feed stream in an infinite calibration loop while the House Brain waits.
Refinement:

The calibration loop needs a hard convergence limit. If the dynamic fill volume adjustment has not produced an in-spec block within N cycles, the Fabricator stops, flags the soil patch to the House Brain, and requests either a new feed from a different Excavator zone or a soil conditioning intervention. The convergence limit is not a failure state — it is the Fabricator correctly identifying that Verification and fill volume adjustment alone cannot fix what is wrong with this soil.
Result:
Hard convergence limit added: N calibration cycles maximum before soil-flag escalation
If limit exceeded: Fabricator stops, flags to House Brain with calibration history
House Brain decides: request new Excavator zone, request conditioning, or reclassify zone
Prevents infinite calibration spiral consuming the entire feed stream
Calibration cycle count added to Fabricator telemetry as a live signal
Refinement 2 — The Anchor Reliability Problem
Gap identified:

The Earth Anvil transmutation is correct — ground anchors let the Fabricator borrow the Earth's mass. But the blueprint assumes the anchor seats properly on every cycle. Loose topsoil, wet ground near the excavation edge, or previously disturbed ground can all prevent the anchor from achieving the friction required to resist compression force. If the anchor slips mid-cycle, the compression force transmits through the chassis instead, and block geometry is corrupted mid-press.
Refinement:

Anchor seating quality must be confirmed before the compression cycle begins — not assumed. The anchor drive motor's current signature at full depth is the confirmation signal. Low resistance at full depth means the anchor is spinning free in loose soil, not gripping. If current draw falls below the seating threshold, the Fabricator relocates the anchor point by a defined offset and retries. If it still cannot achieve seating confidence, it flags ground condition to House Brain. Same logic the Survey Robot applies to sleeve seating confidence — the contact zone confirms its own grip before force is applied.
Result:
Anchor seating confirmation added as mandatory pre-compression step
Motor current signature at full anchor depth is the seating quality signal
Below-threshold current = free-spinning anchor = relocation triggered automatically
If anchor cannot seat after relocation attempts: ground condition flagged to House Brain
New field: anchor_seat_confidence score emitted with every compression record
Compression cycle blocked until anchor_seat_confidence exceeds minimum threshold
Refinement 3 — The Soil Conditioning Gap
Gap identified:

The Gemini session identifies 'add liquid if dry, add heat if wet' as a named transmutation — but the blueprint never specifies how or when conditioning is applied, or what the trigger threshold is. Dynamic fill volume can compensate for density variation within a band. But extremely dry soil will not bind under compression regardless of fill volume. Extremely wet soil will not hold geometry after ejection. Volume adjustment alone cannot fix either extreme.
Refinement:

A simple inline moisture probe on the feed intake provides a two-condition gate before fill begins. If incoming moisture is below the lower threshold, a small onboard conditioning reservoir adds a measured water dose to the mold before fill. If above the upper threshold, compression speed is reduced and a brief drying pass — heat coil or forced air — is applied before pressing. This is not a soil lab. It is a minimum conditioning gate: too dry adds water, too wet slows and dries. The Survey Robot's moisture data for the source zone should also pre-warn the Fabricator before a problematic batch arrives.
Result:
Inline moisture probe added at feed intake — single measurement per fill cycle
Two-condition gate: below lower threshold = add water dose, above upper threshold = drying pass
Conditioning is minimal and binary — not a soil lab, just a boundary enforcer
Survey zone moisture score consumed as a pre-batch warning signal before problematic soil arrives
New fields: incoming_moisture_pct, conditioning_applied (none/water/dry) emitted per cycle
Refinement 4 — The Block Ejection and Handling Problem
Gap identified:

The blueprint defines compression well but says nothing about what happens after the press releases. A freshly-pressed compressed earth block at ejection is at peak vulnerability — binder not cured, block warm, corners fragile. Any lateral force during immediate transfer can chip edges or crack the block face. If the Fabricator hands a fresh block directly to Logistics or Assembly without any dwell, handling damage before Verification even sees it corrupts the quality pipeline.
Refinement:

After press release, the block is held in a support cradle for a defined ejection dwell time before handoff. This is not full cure — it is the minimum dwell for the block to achieve handling strength. The dwell time is a parameter Verification learns over time: if blocks arrive at Verification with fresh-handling damage, dwell is insufficient. Verification feeds the recommended dwell time back to the Fabricator as a tunable parameter, exactly as it feeds fill volume. New field: ejection_dwell_seconds as a Verification-tuned parameter.
Result:
Controlled ejection cradle added — block held in support position after press release
Ejection dwell time: minimum hold before handoff to Logistics/Assembly
Dwell time is a Verification-tuned parameter — not a fixed constant
If Verification sees handling damage on arrival, it increases recommended dwell
New field: ejection_dwell_seconds emitted per fabrication record
New Verification → Fabricator feedback field: recommended_dwell_seconds
Cross-System Refinement — Fabricator Feeds Excavator Feed Quality — Cross-System Data Path
Gap identified:

The Excavator delivers a coarse-qualified feed stream and considers its job done. But the Fabricator is the first system that processes that feed at fine resolution — it knows calibration cycles to convergence, fill volume adjustment required, moisture conditioning applied, and reject rate per batch. All of this is ground-truth quality data about the Excavator's output that the Excavator never receives. The Excavator's binary gate is currently tuned blind.
Refinement:

After each soil batch processed, the Fabricator emits a feed-quality record to the Excavator and House Brain. This record includes calibration cycles to convergence, fill volume delta from nominal, moisture conditioning applied, and batch reject rate. The Excavator uses this to tune its binary gate aperture. If the Fabricator consistently reports high calibration cycles and heavy conditioning from a particular zone, that zone's gate threshold is too permissive — letting through borderline material that should have been rejected. Over builds, the Fabricator's processing difficulty becomes the Excavator's calibration signal, tightening the whole pipeline without any additional sensors.
Result:
New output channel: fabricator_feed_quality_record emitted to Excavator and House Brain after each batch
Record includes: calibration_cycles_to_convergence, fill_volume_delta, conditioning_applied, batch_reject_rate, source_zone_id
Excavator uses feed quality history to tune binary gate aperture per zone
High Fabricator difficulty from zone X → Excavator tightens gate threshold for zone X
Pipeline improves across builds with no additional sensors added to either robot
Excavator blueprint updated: new input channel from Fabricator feed quality records
7. Core Mission
The Fabricator Robot must:
Receive coarse-qualified soil feed from the surface sorting container
Read incoming moisture and apply minimum conditioning if outside threshold
Drive ground anchors and confirm seating before every compression cycle
Fill mold to current fill-volume target
Compress to fixed target geometry using Earth-anchored press force
Hold block in ejection cradle for Verification-tuned dwell time
Hand off completed block to Verification / Logistics
Receive fill volume and dwell time feedback from Verification
Escalate to House Brain if calibration loop exceeds convergence limit
Emit feed quality records to Excavator after each batch
Emit full fabrication telemetry to House Brain
8. Functional Role
Fabricator turns:
Coarse-qualified earth feed
Inline moisture reading
House Brain recipe (target geometry and density class)
Verification feedback (fill volume and dwell corrections)
...into dimensionally consistent, density-verified structural blocks ready for Assembly.
It is the conversion point between raw earth and structural material.
9. System Architecture
9.1 Inputs
From House Brain
Block role and recipe ID
Target geometry (dimensions and density class)
Convergence limit (max calibration cycles before escalation)
Batch routing instructions
From Verification
Block density result: too light / in-spec / too dense
Fill volume correction delta
Recommended ejection dwell seconds
Handling damage flag (triggers dwell increase)
From Surface Container / Excavator
Soil feed stream
Feed rate and availability signal
From Survey
Zone moisture score — pre-batch warning before problematic soil arrives
9.2 Outputs
To Verification / Logistics
Completed block with part ID
Fabrication record: fill volume, compression force, moisture reading, anchor seating score, dwell applied
To House Brain
Block produced / calibration block rejected
Calibration cycle count per batch
Convergence limit flag if exceeded
Anchor seating failure flag
Conditioning applied per cycle
To Excavator
fabricator_feed_quality_record per batch: calibration cycles, fill volume delta, conditioning applied, reject rate, source zone ID
10. Mechanical Architecture
10.1 Baseline Physical Form
Recommended baseline:
Wheeled or tracked mobile base
Ground anchor deployment system — screw or expanding friction type
Mold / press cavity with fixed target geometry
Compression actuator — ball screw, toggle joint, or equivalent high-force low-mass mechanism
Feed hopper intake from surface container
Inline moisture probe at feed intake
Minimal conditioning system — small water reservoir + optional heat/air element
Ejection cradle with dwell timer
Anchor motor with current sensing
This is not a hydraulic press on a truck. It is a field-anchored compression cell that borrows the Earth's mass.
10.2 Ground Anchor System
Purpose: resist compression reaction force without requiring robot mass.
Anchor deployed into undisturbed ground before each compression cycle
Motor current at full depth is the seating quality signal
Below-threshold current: anchor relocates by defined offset and retries
Failed seating after retries: flag to House Brain, do not compress
Key insight:
The robot is not pushing against its own weight. It is pulling itself between the anchor and the press plate. The Earth is the anvil. The Earth is also the strap.
10.3 Compression Mechanism
Fixed mold geometry — target dimensions do not change
Fill volume is the variable parameter, not press depth
Compression force path: ground anchor → compression actuator → press plate → mold → Earth
No force transmitted through robot chassis
10.4 Moisture Conditioning System
Two-condition gate — not a soil lab:
Below lower moisture threshold: water dose added to mold before fill
Above upper moisture threshold: drying pass (heat or forced air) before press
Within range: no conditioning — proceed directly
Water reservoir sized for a defined number of conditioning cycles before refill
10.5 Ejection Cradle
Block held in support cradle after press release
Dwell timer controlled by Verification feedback parameter
No lateral force on block during dwell
Handoff to Logistics/Assembly only after dwell complete
11. Sensor Stack
11.1 Required Sensors
Anchor and Ground
Anchor drive motor current sensor — seating confidence
Anchor depth encoder
Feed and Conditioning
Inline moisture probe at feed intake
Fill volume encoder / hopper weight
Water dose meter (if conditioning applied)
Compression
Compression force estimate — actuator current or load cell
Press depth encoder
Mold release sensor — confirms block ejection
Positioning
IMU
Wheel/track encoders
UWB / site localization
11.2 Minimum Sensed Outputs Per Cycle
Block part ID
Fill volume used
Anchor seat confidence score
Incoming moisture pct
Conditioning applied
Compression force achieved
Press depth reached
Ejection dwell seconds applied
Calibration cycle count for current batch
Decision: production block or calibration block
12. Software Architecture
12.1 Main Modules
Survey moisture pre-warning consumer
Feed intake moisture checker and conditioning gate
Anchor deployment and seating confirmation module
Fill volume controller — current target maintained and updated from Verification
Compression cycle controller
Ejection cradle and dwell timer
Calibration loop manager — convergence counter and limit enforcer
Verification feedback receiver — fill volume and dwell corrections
Feed quality record emitter — to Excavator after each batch
Telemetry emitter — to House Brain
12.2 Fabricator Doctrine
Stage 0 — Pre-Batch Warning
Consume Survey zone moisture score for incoming feed. If above slurry threshold, pre-arm the conditioning gate and notify House Brain before the first fill cycle.
Stage 1 — Moisture Check and Conditioning
Read inline moisture at feed intake. Apply conditioning if outside bounds: water dose if too dry, drying pass if too wet. Record conditioning_applied for telemetry.
Stage 2 — Anchor and Confirm

Deploy ground anchor. Read motor current at full depth. Confirm seating confidence exceeds threshold. Relocate and retry if insufficient. Block compression until confirmed. Do not compress on a floating anchor.
Stage 3 — Fill
Load mold to current fill_volume_target. Fill volume is the only parameter adjusted by the calibration loop — geometry target does not move.
Stage 4 — Compress
Drive actuator to target press depth. Force path runs through anchor and Earth, not chassis. Record compression force and depth.
Stage 5 — Eject and Dwell
Release press. Transfer block to ejection cradle. Hold for ejection_dwell_seconds. No handoff until dwell complete.
Stage 6 — Hand Off
Transfer block to Verification / Logistics with fabrication record. Await Verification result.
Stage 7 — Calibration Update

Receive Verification feedback: density result, fill volume correction delta, recommended dwell. Update fill_volume_target and ejection_dwell_seconds for next cycle. Increment calibration cycle counter if block was rejected.
Stage 8 — Convergence Check
If calibration_cycle_count exceeds convergence limit: stop pressing, flag soil batch to House Brain with full calibration history. Wait for instruction — do not continue on bad soil.
Stage 9 — Feed Quality Emit
After each batch, emit fabricator_feed_quality_record to Excavator and House Brain: calibration cycles, fill volume delta, conditioning applied, reject rate, source zone ID.
13. Calibration Loop Detail
The calibration loop is the core of Fabricator's intelligence. It is not a sensor system. It is a feedback cycle.
The invariant rule:
Target geometry never changes. Fill volume is the only knob. Assembly's dimensional requirement is sacred. Everything else bends to it.
Calibration States
too_light → increase fill volume by delta
in_spec → no change, reset calibration counter
too_dense / overloaded → decrease fill volume by delta
calibration_limit_exceeded → stop, flag, request new feed or intervention
Calibration Block Policy
First 3–5 blocks of any new soil patch are expected calibration blocks
Calibration blocks are recycled back into the feed hopper — not wasted
Calibration cycle count resets on each new identified soil patch
Calibration block count is reported to House Brain so pipeline scheduling can account for it
Convergence Signal to Excavator
Batches that converge in 1–2 cycles: gate threshold is well-tuned
Batches that need 5+ cycles: gate is too permissive for this zone
Batches that hit convergence limit: flag zone as poor quality feed source
14. Commands
"commands": [
"load_recipe(recipe_id)",
"check_feed_moisture()",
"apply_conditioning(type, dose)",
"deploy_anchor()",
"confirm_anchor_seating()",
"relocate_anchor(offset)",
"fill_mold(volume_target)",
"compress()",
"eject_block()",
"begin_dwell(dwell_seconds)",
"release_to_verification(part_id)",
"update_fill_volume(delta)",
"update_dwell_seconds(value)",
"flag_convergence_limit(batch_id)",
"emit_fabrication_record(part_id)",
"emit_feed_quality_record(batch_id)",
"recycle_calibration_block(part_id)",
"return_to_base()",
"abort_job(reason)"
15. Data Emitted
15.1 Fabrication Record — Per Block
"part_id": "block_244",
"recipe_id": "ext_wall_v3",
"batch_id": "batch_18",
"fill_volume_used_L": 4.31,
"fill_volume_target_L": 4.28,
"incoming_moisture_pct": 11.2,
"conditioning_applied": "none",
"anchor_seat_confidence": 0.91,
"compression_force_kN": 142.0,
"press_depth_mm": 150,
"ejection_dwell_seconds": 45,
"calibration_cycle_count": 2,
"block_type": "production",
15.2 Feed Quality Record — Per Batch, Emitted to Excavator
"batch_id": "batch_18",
"source_zone_id": "siteprep_a1",
"calibration_cycles_to_convergence": 3,
"fill_volume_delta_from_nominal": 0.12,
"conditioning_applied": "water_dose",
"batch_reject_rate": 0.08,
"convergence_limit_hit": false,
"assessment": "gate_slightly_permissive",
15.3 Anchor Failure Record
"event": "anchor_seating_failed",
"zone_id": "siteprep_a1",
"anchor_attempts": 3,
"motor_current_at_depth": 0.31,
"seating_threshold": 0.65,
"ground_condition": "loose_disturbed",
"action": "flagged_to_house_brain",
16. Failure Modes
Anchor-Side Failures
Anchor fails to seat in loose or wet ground
Anchor seating confidence below threshold after relocation attempts
Anchor seizes mid-cycle — compression force transmitted to chassis
Anchor retraction failure after compression
Feed and Conditioning Failures
Incoming moisture probe failure — conditioning gate blind
Moisture too extreme for two-condition gate to compensate
Water reservoir empty — conditioning unavailable
Feed hopper clog — fill volume inconsistent
Calibration Failures
Calibration loop fails to converge — soil patch too variable
Convergence limit hit — soil flagged, production stops
Verification feedback delayed — fill volume target stale
Fill volume correction overshoots — oscillation in calibration loop
Compression and Ejection Failures
Compression force insufficient — actuator underperforms
Block sticks in mold — ejection incomplete
Block damaged in cradle — dwell too short
Ejection cradle misalignment — block corners exposed to lateral force
System-Side Failures
Wrong recipe loaded
Stale Survey moisture data — conditioning mismatch
Feed quality record not emitted — Excavator gate calibration gap
Verification feedback channel broken — fill volume target frozen
17. Success Criteria
Fabricator is successful if it can:
Produce dimensionally consistent blocks from variable soil feed
Confirm anchor seating before every compression cycle — never compress on a floating anchor
Apply minimum conditioning without becoming a soil lab
Converge the calibration loop within the defined limit on normal soil
Correctly escalate soil batches that exceed convergence limit rather than looping indefinitely
Protect freshly-pressed blocks in the ejection cradle until handling strength is reached
Receive Verification feedback and update fill volume and dwell without human intervention
Emit feed quality records that allow the Excavator to tune its gate over time
18. Environment Assumptions
Fabricator assumes:
Surface sorting container provides a coarse-qualified soil feed
Ground is suitable for anchor deployment within the work zone — not solid bedrock, not fully saturated
Verification is available at end of each production or calibration cycle
House Brain provides recipe and convergence limit before production begins
Survey has provided zone moisture data before the first feed batch arrives
Excavator has an input channel to receive feed quality records
19. Build Phases
Phase 1 — Static Compression Bench
Build:
Ground anchor system
Fixed mold geometry
Compression actuator
Anchor seating confirmation via motor current
Goal: prove Earth Anvil principle — compress to spec without machine mass.
Phase 2 — Fill Volume Calibration Loop
Add:
Fill volume encoder
Verification feedback receiver
Dynamic fill volume update logic
Calibration cycle counter
Goal: prove fill volume loop converges on variable test soil within N cycles.
Phase 3 — Moisture Conditioning Gate
Add:
Inline moisture probe
Water conditioning reservoir
Two-condition gate logic
Survey moisture pre-warning consumer
Goal: prove Fabricator handles soil at the dry and wet extremes without calibration loop failure.
Phase 4 — Ejection Cradle and Dwell
Add:
Ejection support cradle
Dwell timer
Verification-tuned dwell feedback
Goal: prove blocks arrive at Verification without handling damage.
Phase 5 — Convergence Limit and Escalation
Add:
Convergence limit enforcement
Soil patch flagging to House Brain
Calibration block recycling path
Goal: prove Fabricator cannot be trapped in an infinite calibration spiral.
Phase 6 — Feed Quality Emit and House Brain Integration
Connect:
Feed quality record channel to Excavator
Full House Brain command and telemetry loop
Zone navigation for multi-position press operation
Goal: make Fabricator a full participant in the system feedback loop — not just a press.
20. Cost Direction
Fabricator stays cheap by:
Using Earth mass instead of machine mass for compression force
Using a ball screw or toggle joint instead of a multi-ton hydraulic frame
Replacing an onboard soil lab with a two-condition moisture gate
Replacing quality intelligence with a feedback tether to Verification
Keeping geometry fixed and only tuning fill volume — one variable instead of many
Using the Survey Robot's existing moisture data rather than new sensors
C2A Principle:
Borrow the Earth's mass. Fix the shape. Tune the fill. Let Verification be the brain. Feed what you learned back to Excavator.
21. Out of Scope / Not This Robot's Job
Fabricator does not:
Determine final recipe suitability or structural role
Certify block quality — that is Verification's job
Dig or classify soil — that is Excavator's job
Transport finished blocks to the build site — that is Logistics
Place blocks — that is Assembly
Those belong to: Excavator, Verification, Logistics, Assembly, and House Brain.
Fabricator only says: "Given this earth feed and this recipe, I can produce dimensionally consistent blocks and converge on the correct density with Verification's guidance."
22. Final Blueprint Summary

The Fabricator Robot is a ground-anchored, earth-pressing machine that ingests the Excavator's coarse-qualified soil feed, applies minimum moisture conditioning via a two-condition gate, confirms anchor seating before every compression cycle, compresses to a fixed geometry using the Earth as the anvil, tunes fill volume dynamically through a Verification feedback loop bounded by a hard convergence limit, protects freshly-pressed blocks in an ejection cradle, and feeds batch quality data back to the Excavator to improve its gate calibration across all future builds.
